planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil has warmed to at least 65°F; in zone 9–12 pigeon pea grows as a short-lived perennial woody shrub and may produce pods for 2–5 years before needing replacement. In cooler zones grow as an annual and start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in well-drained, low-fertility soil in full sun after soil warms to 65°F; pigeon pea is a deep-rooted, drought-tolerant shrubby legume that is one of the world's most important tropical food crops — the yellow, green, or red seeds (arhar dal, toor dal) are a dietary staple across the Caribbean, South Asia, and sub-Saharan Africa, used in dals, stews, rice dishes, and soups. The plants fix atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules and thrive in poor soils without supplemental fertilizer; overly rich or wet soils reduce pod production. Harvest green pods when plump for fresh use or allow to fully dry on the plant for dried dal; in zone 9–12 plants become woody perennial shrubs and will continue producing pods for several years if pruned back hard after the main harvest to stimulate new growth. In zone 8 and below, grow as a warm-season annual and expect a single late-season harvest.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; provide a sturdy trellis or fence as vines grow rapidly to 10–15 feet. In zones 9–11 hyacinth bean may overwinter as a perennial; elsewhere grow as a vigorous warm-season annual.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep at the base of a sturdy trellis, fence, or arbor after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; hyacinth bean is a fast-climbing, ornamental tropical vine producing deep purple stems, lavender to violet pea flowers in upright racemes, and shiny deep-purple seed pods that are among the most spectacular of any annual vine. Young pods and fresh seeds are edible when thoroughly cooked — raw or undercooked seeds contain toxic cyanogenic glycosides (phytohaemagglutinin) and must never be eaten without boiling; immature flat pods may be eaten like snap beans after thorough cooking. The vine reaches 10–15 feet in a single season and thrives in hot, humid summer conditions where it fixes nitrogen and provides dense ornamental coverage on structures. In tropical and subtropical climates (zone 9–11), plants may persist as perennials and develop a woody base.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; tepary bean requires warmth and actually performs poorly in cool, wet conditions. In the desert Southwest, traditional plantings coincide with the summer monsoon season.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in very well-drained, low-fertility, sandy or rocky soil in full sun well after soil has warmed; tepary bean is the most drought-tolerant domesticated bean in the world, an ancient crop of the Sonoran Desert developed over thousands of years by the O'odham and other indigenous peoples of the American Southwest and northern Mexico. Plants complete their entire life cycle — from germination through harvest — on as little as 8 inches of rainfall and perform poorly in heavily irrigated or rich, wet soils where they become disease-prone. Both bush and semi-vining types exist; small seeds come in white, brown, tan, buff, and speckled patterns and are high in protein, soluble fiber, and resistant starch. Harvest entire plants when most pods have dried and turned tan, thresh to release seeds, and cure in a dry, well-ventilated location before storage. An outstanding crop for hot, dry garden conditions where common beans fail.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to light shade. Lightly scarify the hard seed coat or soak overnight in warm water before planting to improve germination. Hog Peanut thrives in the dappled light of woodland edges and will not perform well in exposed, dry, or sunny conditions.

directions: Direct sow in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial shade after last frost, lightly covering seeds with soil; hog peanut is a remarkable native annual vine of eastern North American forest floors and woodland edges that produces two entirely different types of seeds — small, lavender pea flowers on climbing aerial stems that rarely set fruit, and inconspicuous cleistogamous (self-fertilizing) flowers that develop into fleshy, one-seeded pods just below or at the soil surface. The subterranean seeds are sweet, nutritious, and bean-like in flavor; they were an important food for many indigenous peoples including the Cherokee, Meskwaki, and Iroquois, and settlers named the plant hog peanut because pigs would eagerly root them from the soil. As a nitrogen-fixing native legume it enriches woodland soils while growing happily in conditions that exclude most garden vegetables. Plants self-seed and can form spreading colonies in moist, shaded naturalistic plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in lean, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; trailing wild bean is adapted to dry, sandy, open habitats including beach dunes, prairie margins, and sandy roadsides. Scarifying the hard seed coat before planting improves germination speed.

directions: Direct sow in lean, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost, lightly scarifying the hard seed coat to improve germination; trailing wild bean is a native annual legume vine of prairies, sandy open woodlands, beach dunes, and disturbed sandy soils across eastern North America. The small, pink to rose-purple pea flowers, produced throughout summer and into fall, are visited by native bees including specialist legume bees, and the slender bean pods that follow are consumed by Northern Bobwhite, Wild Turkey, and other ground-feeding birds. As a nitrogen-fixing native annual it builds soil fertility on sandy, nutrient-poor sites where few other plants will grow and self-sows reliably to maintain itself in open, sunny, sandy areas year after year. The trailing and twining stems weave through low grasses and shrubs, creating naturalistic low cover that is excellent for dry prairie restoration, sandy slope stabilization, and wildlife-focused native garden plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil temperature reaches 68°F or warmer; jack bean is a tropical legume that requires a long, warm growing season of at least 4–5 months and performs best in zone 9–12 where the season allows full pod and seed development.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, low- to moderate-fertility soil in full sun 2–4 weeks after last frost once soil is thoroughly warm; jack bean is a vigorous tropical legume grown throughout the humid tropics and subtropics as a cover crop, green manure, and food legume. The large, upright to semi-climbing plants produce conspicuous rose-purple flowers followed by flat, strap-like pods 8–14 inches long containing large white seeds used in soups, stews, and curries in parts of Africa, Central America, and Southeast Asia — caution: seeds are toxic unless thoroughly cooked due to the alkaloid canavanine and must be boiled in multiple changes of water before eating. As a cover crop, jack bean fixes significant atmospheric nitrogen, smothers weeds with its dense foliage, and tolerates poor, acid, and lateritic soils that challenge other legumes. Terminate as a cover crop by mowing or incorporation at early pod set to maximize the nitrogen benefit to subsequent crops.
